food_subgroup: Dry spiced potato and cauliflower curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Aloo Gobi. Nearest parent is a mixed vegetable curry with root vegetables. A new concept for North Indian dry vegetable curries is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dry spiced potato and cauliflower vegetable dish, North Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of aloo gobi is 1 bowl (approximately 200g) — a semi-dry curry of diced potato and cauliflower florets spiced with turmeric, cumin, and garam masala, served as a side with roti or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Gobi — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000002
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN under entry EI000002 — Aloo Gobi, standard North Indian dry preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked potato and cauliflower mixture; the dry-cooked cauliflower significantly moderates the GI compared to boiled potato alone.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 396mg per bowl (200g) — moderate; the very high potassium (796mg per bowl) from potatoes and cauliflower is outstanding for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — potatoes and cauliflower together provide exceptional potassium content. The dish is naturally low in sodium in home cooking; restaurant versions may have more added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) for the combination; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 16. The cauliflower significantly moderates the GI of the potato.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aloo gobi is acceptable for diabetic patients in moderate portions — the cauliflower provides fibre and vitamin C that moderate the potato's glycaemic impact. Limit to 150g portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cauliflower is one of the richest vegetable sources of vitamin C (32mg/100g) and contains anti-inflammatory sulforaphane precursors; turmeric provides curcumin; potatoes provide potassium and vitamin B6.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Cauliflower's vitamin C and sulforaphane compound have documented anti-inflammatory effects. Potatoes are sometimes avoided in Nightshade-free diets, which some RA patients follow — individualize accordingly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobi is a simple yet iconic North Indian dry vegetable curry — diced potatoes and cauliflower florets cooked together in a wok or kadai with cumin seeds, turmeric, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and green chilli until tender and lightly golden. Unlike most Indian curries, aloo gobi is semi-dry — the vegetables are coated in spices rather than swimming in gravy. It is vegan, gluten-free, and exceptionally rich in vitamin C, potassium, and vitamin B6. A staple in North Indian restaurants and a popular accompaniment to chapati.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under entry EI000002 — Aloo Gobi, standard North Indian preparation.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Cholesterol is 0.0. Exceptionally high vitamin C (32mg/100g) from cauliflower and potatoes — 64mg per bowl (200g) covers 71% of the daily recommended intake. Potassium is very high from potatoes (approximately 796mg per bowl). Some rheumatoid arthritis patients avoid nightshade vegetables (potatoes) — individualize advice.
